policy_name: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 - Access Control (AC) Family
purpose: Apply NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Access Control family controls to classify permissions as Privileged or Risky. Focuses on AC-2 (Account Management), AC-3 (Access Enforcement), AC-5 (Separation of Duties), AC-6 (Least Privilege), and AC-17 (Remote Access).

classification_model.privileged: Under AC-6, a permission is Privileged when it exceeds the minimum access required for a principal's role. Privileged access must be explicitly authorized, logged, and reviewed periodically.
classification_model.risky: A permission is Risky when it violates AC-3 (access enforcement) by granting access beyond what the security policy allows, or when it creates conditions for AC-5 (separation of duties) violations.

rules.name: Service Account with Interactive / User-Level Permissions
rules.description: NIST AC-2 requires organisations to identify and manage accounts by type (individual, shared, group, system, service). Service accounts (non-human) should never hold interactive or user-level permissions. A service account granted admin, delete, or policy-management actions violates account type boundaries.
rules.nist_control: AC-2(1) - Automated System Account Management; AC-2(6) - Dynamic Privilege Management
rules.rationale: Service accounts are often shared and lack MFA. Granting them elevated permissions creates a privileged pathway that bypasses user-level controls.
rules.remediation: Scope service account permissions to the minimum data-plane actions required. Use workload identity federation instead of long-lived credentials.

rules.name: Wildcard Resource with Allow Effect
rules.description: AC-3 requires that access to resources be enforced in accordance with the approved authorisations. A wildcard resource ('*') bypasses resource-level enforcement entirely, granting access to all current and future resources of a type.
rules.nist_control: AC-3 - Access Enforcement; AC-3(7) - Role-Based Access Control
rules.rationale: Wildcard resources violate the principle of explicit authorisation. Resources added after the policy is written are automatically in scope, which can never be explicitly authorised.
rules.remediation: Replace '*' with a specific resource ARN, path, or name. Use resource tags or conditions to scope access where exact names are dynamic.

rules.name: Combined Read-Policy and Write-Policy on Same Principal
rules.description: AC-5 requires that duties and areas of responsibility be separated to reduce the risk of malevolent activity. A principal who can both read the current policy AND write/replace it can silently modify their own permissions — a classic separation-of-duties violation.

triggers.combined_actions_description: Same principal holds both read-policy and write-policy actions

rules.nist_control: AC-5(a) - Separation of Duties; AC-5(b) - Document Separation of Duties
rules.rationale: A principal who can read and write their own policy can grant themselves any additional permission without oversight — a privilege escalation path.
rules.remediation: Split policy-read (for audit) and policy-write (for change management) into separate roles assigned to separate principals or teams.

rules.name: Wildcard Action (Any Action)
rules.description: AC-6 mandates that principals are granted only the authorisations needed to accomplish required tasks. A wildcard action ('*' or ':*') grants all current and future actions, which can never satisfy least privilege.
rules.nist_control: AC-6 - Least Privilege; AC-6(1) - Authorise Access to Security Functions; AC-6(5) - Privileged Accounts
rules.rationale: Wildcard actions guarantee over-provisioning. They grant every new API action automatically, meaning the effective permissions grow over time without any authorisation event.
rules.remediation: Enumerate the exact actions required by the workload. Use AWS Access Advisor, GCP Policy Insights, or Azure Access Review to identify which actions are actually used.

rules.name: Administrative or Owner-Level Permission
rules.description: AC-6(5) requires that organisations restrict privileged accounts (those with administrator, superuser, or owner access) to a minimum and subject them to heightened controls. Any permission containing admin, owner, FullAccess, root, or superuser keywords is classified as Privileged.
rules.nist_control: AC-6(5) - Privileged Accounts; AC-6(9) - Log Use of Privileged Functions
rules.rationale: Administrative accounts represent the highest-risk access class. Compromise of a single admin account can result in total resource takeover.
rules.remediation: Replace managed admin policies with custom policies granting only the specific actions needed. Use AWS Permission Boundaries or Azure PIM to time-bound privileged access.

rules.name: Org-Level or Account-Wide Scope
rules.description: A permission scoped to the entire organisation, account, or management group grants access to all resources within that scope — present and future. Even read-only access at this level is Privileged because it exposes the full inventory of resources.
rules.nist_control: AC-6 - Least Privilege; AC-6(3) - Network Access to Privileged Commands
rules.rationale: Account-wide scope means the permission applies to resources the granting administrator may not be aware of yet. Every new resource is automatically in scope.
rules.remediation: Scope permissions to specific resource groups, projects, or named resources. Use SCPs/Organisation Policies to enforce resource-level scoping.

rules.name: Cross-Account or Cross-Organisation Trust without Condition
rules.description: AC-17 requires that organisations authorise, monitor, and control all remote access to the system. A trust relationship that allows a principal from an external account or organisation to assume a role without an ExternalId, MFA, or Org condition is an uncontrolled remote access path.
rules.nist_control: AC-17(1) - Automated Monitoring and Control; AC-17(2) - Protection of Confidentiality and Integrity Using Encryption
rules.rationale: Cross-account access without a verifiable condition (ExternalId, Org, MFA) is exploitable via confused-deputy attacks. Any account that knows the role ARN can assume it.
rules.remediation: Add aws:PrincipalOrgID to limit assumption to principals within your organisation. Add sts:ExternalId for third-party integrations. Enforce MFA with condition key aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent.
